title: How to Hit a Consistent Tennis Serve
description: The serve is the only shot in tennis you fully control. Learn the mechanics that make it reliable — not just powerful.

steps.title: Start with the continental grip
steps.instruction: Hold the racket like you're shaking hands with the edge of the frame — the 'chopper' grip. Your index knuckle should sit on bevel 2 (the top-right bevel for right-handers). This is the only grip for a consistent serve.
steps.tip: If your palm is flat on the back of the handle, you're using the wrong grip.

media.caption: Tennis serve mechanics step by step

steps.title: Stand sideways to the baseline
steps.instruction: Position your front foot at a 45-degree angle to the baseline, back foot parallel to the baseline. This sideways stance coils your body for rotation — like loading a spring.
steps.tip: Your front foot should point toward the right net post (for right-handers serving from the deuce court).

steps.title: Toss with your non-dominant arm only
steps.instruction: The toss arm should stay straight throughout. Lift — don't throw — the ball from waist height. Release it at eye level and let it float up. No spin, no wrist flip.
steps.tip: Practice the toss alone: catch it back in your hand without swinging. It should land in the same spot every time.

steps.title: Toss slightly in front and to your right
steps.instruction: For a flat serve, toss 1 o'clock position (in front of your right shoulder). This positions the ball so you can swing through it efficiently at full arm extension.
steps.tip: A toss that's too far behind will cause you to arch back and lose power.

steps.title: The trophy position: scratch your back
steps.instruction: As the ball rises, bring your racket behind your head like you're about to scratch between your shoulder blades. Elbow up, racket head dropping down behind. This is the 'trophy position' — the loaded position.
steps.tip: Don't rush this — let the ball dictate your timing.

steps.title: Drive up with your legs
steps.instruction: Bend your knees as you load, then explode upward as you swing. The power in a serve comes from your legs and torso rotation, not your arm. Think 'up and through', not 'swing sideways'.
steps.tip: You should feel like you're jumping toward the ball.

steps.title: Hit at full arm extension — reach for the sky
steps.instruction: Contact the ball at the highest point you can reach with a straight arm. The higher the contact point, the better the angle into the service box. Think of trying to touch the ceiling.
steps.tip: Most beginners hit too low. Consciously try to hit higher.

steps.title: Pronate your wrist at contact
steps.instruction: At the moment of impact, rotate your forearm inward (pronation) so your palm faces the ground after contact. This adds speed and is what prevents elbow injuries on the serve.
steps.tip: If you're slapping the ball, you're not pronating enough.

steps.title: Follow through across your body
steps.instruction: After contact, let your arm swing naturally across to your left hip (for right-handers). Don't stop the racket abruptly. A full follow-through is what transfers energy efficiently and protects your shoulder.
steps.tip: Your racket should end up pointing to your left foot.

steps.title: Land inside the court and recover
steps.instruction: Let your momentum carry your back foot forward so you land inside the baseline in an athletic ready position. You're now prepared for the return. A serve that leaves you off-balance wastes any advantage you gained.
steps.tip: Focus on consistency before power — a 60% first serve beats a 20% bomb.
